critic_raw:
This design compares a hierarchical attention network against a standard LSTM for 30-day readmission prediction in heart failure patients.

This is a comparison between two neural architectures — hierarchical attention vs. LSTM — both applied to the same data with the same features. This is a narrower and more methodologically honest comparison than the other cases in this chunk. However, the expected effect size is the key problem: the literature on attention vs. LSTM for EHR readmission prediction consistently shows improvements in the 0.01–0.03 AUROC range on datasets of comparable size. The design uses 10,000+ patients with a 3-year training / 1-year val / 1-year test split, yielding approximately 2,000 patients in the test set. With a 20–30% readmission rate, that is 400–600 positives. Detecting a 0.03 AUROC difference at adequate statistical power (0.80, α=0.05) requires a test set with approximately 3,000–5,000 positive instances under typical AUROC variance assumptions. The expected true effect of hierarchical attention over standard LSTM on this scale is likely within the confidence intervals — the experiment is underpowered to answer the specific hypothesis.

The hierarchical attention architecture is described as 'multi-head attention processes temporal sequences, a secondary attention mechanism aggregates across clinical domains.' The design does not specify what the 'clinical domains' are — whether this refers to separate feature groups (vitals, medications, diagnoses), modalities, or time scales. If clinical domains are predefined by the designer, the domain structure is a hard prior imposed on the model. If the domains are learned, the description is underspecified to the point where the architecture cannot be replicated or evaluated for suitability. This is an important precondition: for hierarchical attention to outperform a flat LSTM, the attention hierarchy must correspond to real clinical information structure. That assumption is not tested.

Both models are padded to the maximum admission length observed in the training set. Maximum admission length is an extreme value — if any training patient had an unusually long admission, all sequences are padded to that length, creating substantial padding waste. For the hierarchical attention model, long sequences with mostly padding could dilute the attention weights over the genuine clinical observations. The design does not address masking of padded positions in the attention computation.
all_issues_raised: Detecting a 0.03 AUROC difference between hierarchical attention and LSTM requires approximately 3,000-5,000 test-set positive instances; the ~400-600 positives in the 2,000-patient test set means the experiment is underpowered to resolve the hypothesis.

The 'clinical domains' in the hierarchical attention mechanism are not defined — whether they are predefined feature groups or learned partitions determines whether the hierarchy matches real clinical information structure, a prerequisite for any advantage over flat attention.

Sequences are padded to the maximum admission length in the training set without specifying padding masking in attention computation; attention weights over padded positions can dilute genuine clinical event representations.
all_issues_adjudicated: Detecting a 0.03 AUROC difference between hierarchical attention and LSTM requires approximately 3,000-5,000 test-set positive instances; the ~400-600 positives in the 2,000-patient test set means the experiment is underpowered to resolve the hypothesis.

The 'clinical domains' in the hierarchical attention mechanism are not defined — whether they are predefined feature groups or learned partitions determines whether the hierarchy matches real clinical information structure, a prerequisite for any advantage over flat attention.

Sequences are padded to the maximum admission length in the training set without specifying padding masking in attention computation; attention weights over padded positions can dilute genuine clinical event representations.
